retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Genetic predisposition is associated with mutations affecting regulators of vascular calcification, such as MGP or ectonucleotide pyrophosphatase/phosphodiesterase 1 (ENPP1), which can predispose individuals to accelerated arterial calcification.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intimal CAC begins in the atherosclerotic core. The death of macrophages in the atheromatous core serves as a nucleating site for the formation of calcium deposits. The dying macrophages or smooth muscle cells release extracellular vesicles, which provide the scaffolding for developing calcium deposits. Reduced expression of mineralization inhibitors, otherwise produced by intact macrophages and vascular smooth muscle cells, occurs. Calcium-phosphate metabolism control is lost. Calcium-phosphate concentration in autophagosomes rises and forms calcium-phosphate crystals, which can be seen through electron microscopy. Macroscopic CAC evolves from these foci. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Intimal CAC resembles endochondral bone formation. In intimal CAC, most chondrocyte-like cells originate from the bone marrow with a minimal contribution from the differentiation of local smooth muscle cells into chondrocytes. In contrast, medial CAC is driven mainly by medial smooth muscle cells undergoing osteochondrogenic differentiation. Inflammatory mediators and mechanical stress drive the transition of vascular smooth muscle cells into osteoblast-like cells. These cells express osteogenic markers like bone morphogenetic proteins (BMPs) and Runx2, which are involved in bone formation. This osteogenic process leads to the formation of hydroxyapatite crystals, the same material found in bones, within the atherosclerotic plaque. The lesion progresses with the cytokines produced by the chondrocyte-like cells. [11]

retrieved_docs.title: Calciphylaxis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Deficiency of vascular calcification inhibitors such as fetuin-A, osteoprotegerin, and matrix G1a protein may play a role in the development of calciphylaxis. Fetuin-A is a glycoprotein that binds calcium and phosphorus and may help to prevent calcification of vessels and soft tissue. [5] Fetuin-A is downregulated in dialysis patients. Matrix G1a protein may also prevent vascular calcification and is dependent on vitamin K dependent carboxylation for its activity. Warfarin use has been implicated as a risk factor for calciphylaxis, which may be related to its interference with vitamin K dependent activation of matrix G1a. [6]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic inflammation , such as  rheumatoid arthritis and systemic lupus erythematosus, is associated with increased inflammatory cytokines that activate vascular smooth muscle cells and osteogenic pathways, increasing calcium deposition in arterial walls.

retrieved_docs.title: Hyperphosphatemia -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: In chronic kidney disease (CKD) patients, cardiovascular disease is the most common cause of death, due in part to excess vascular calcification. Amongst dialysis patients, vascular calcification is detected in more than 80 percent of patients by computed tomography (CT) scan. Hyperphosphatemia, along with hypercalcemia and high PTH concentration, induces a phenotypic switch of vascular smooth muscle cells to osteoblast-like cells and local inflammation, leading to medial calcification. Microcalcifications of coronary arteries may increase the vulnerability of the atheromatous plaque to mechanical stress imposed by blood pressure. Calcification of large vessels like the aorta increases arterial stiffness, causing hypertension and increased pulse pressure. [21] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Calcifications -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Two distinct pathological mechanisms for calcifications anywhere in the body are dystrophic calcifications, calcification of dead and dying tissues and metastatic calcification, calcification of normal living tissue. Metastatic calcification is usually related to disturbances in homeostasis of calcium. While dystrophic calcification is not related to any disturbance in calcium metabolism and can occur with normal calcium and phosphorous levels in the blood. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Porcelain Aorta -- Pathophysiology -- Nonatherosclerotic Porcelain Aorta
retrieved_docs.content: In this type, calcification occurs in the tunica media of the aortic wall in the absence of atherosclerosis. It is induced by vascular inflammation, radiation, and uremia via triggering a metaplastic transformation of VSMCs into osteoblasts leading to the production of bone-associated proteins such as alkaline phosphatase, bone sialoprotein, bone GLa protein, and bone morphogenic protein 2. In cases of vascular inflammation and aging, apoptotic vesicles arising from dead VSMCs, and elastin degradation mediated by matrix metalloprotease will act as a nidus for medial calcification by sending a paracrine osteogenic signal that will provoke the aortic calcium deposition as well as the collagen deposition and loss of the elastic fibers causing an arterial wall stiffness. [1] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Chronic kidney disease is  a significant cause of medical CAC. The kidneys have impaired phosphate excretion, leading to hyperphosphatemia. This excess phosphate combines with calcium to form deposits in the walls of arteries, including the coronary arteries. Additionally, chronic kidney disease (CKD) leads to elevated levels of parathyroid hormone (PTH) and fibroblast growth factor-23 (FGF-23), both of which promote vascular calcification. Deficiency of calcification inhibitors, such as fetuin-A and matrix Gla protein (MGP), is also common in CKD patients, further promoting calcification.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Diabetes accelerates both intimal and medial CAC through the combined effects of hyperglycemia, oxidative stress, and inflammation. Advanced glycation end-products contribute to endothelial dysfunction, inflammation, and vascular smooth muscle cell transformation into osteoblast-like cells, promoting calcification.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Hyperparathyroidism  is  associated with excess circulating PTH, which promotes bone resorption and increases serum calcium and phosphate levels, which can then deposit in the arteries and promote calcification.

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The 2 distinct types of CAC are atherosclerotic calcification and calcification of the tunica media. Atherosclerotic CAC mainly affects the tunica intima. The main steps of intimal CAC include endothelial dysfunction, lipid accumulation and inflammation, plaque formation, smooth muscle cell activation, osteogenic differentiation, and calcium deposition. In medial CAC, significant steps include vascular smooth muscle cell differentiation, matrix vesicle release, disruption in calcium-phosphate homeostasis, extracellular matrix remodeling, chronic low-grade inflammation, and arterial stiffness.

retrieved_docs.title: Calciphylaxis -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: The cause and mechanisms leading to calciphylaxis remain poorly understood, and its development is likely dependent on multiple factors that lead to medial calcification of arterioles. Elevated calcium x phosphate product, increased parathyroid hormone levels, and administration of activated vitamin D have been associated with the development of calciphylaxis. However, abnormalities of bone-mineral parameters such as these are typically not sufficient to cause calciphylaxis on their own in most patients. Disturbances in bone-mineral parameters are extremely common in dialysis patients. However, most do not develop calciphylaxis. Further, calciphylaxis may develop even if parathyroid hormone, phosphorus, and calcium levels are normal.

retrieved_docs.title: Chronic Kidney Disease-Mineral Bone Disorder (CKD-MBD) -- Pathophysiology -- Extraskeletal Manifestations
retrieved_docs.content: Vascular calcification: Vascular calcification in CKD-MBD has been extensively studied due to its association with morbidity and mortality. Previously, vascular calcification was considered a passive consequence of degenerative aging, but it is now known to be an actively regulated process similar to bone formation. The process involves complex interactions among vascular smooth muscle cells, the extracellular matrix, and calciprotein particles (CPPs). These particles, formed in high calcium and phosphate conditions, contain calcium, phosphate, fetuin-A, and other proteins. CPPs induce inflammatory responses and can cause smooth muscle cell apoptosis and extracellular matrix calcification. [11] [45] In the uremic milieu, characteristic of CKD, calcification promoters such as calcium and phosphate increase, whereas calcification inhibitors such as fetuin-A and pyrophosphate decrease. [46] Additionally, decreased calcitriol levels are associated with endothelial dysfunction and increased vascular stiffness. [47]

retrieved_docs.title: Coronary Artery Calcification -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Calcium is the most abundant mineral in the human body. Although most calcium is found in teeth and bone, approximately 1% is dissolved in the bloodstream. As the human body ages, calcium is deposited in various body parts. Arterial calcification is closely related to vascular injury, inflammation, and repair. Calcification occurs very early in atherosclerosis; it is only detectable through imaging modalities when it accumulates in tissue and vasculature. This accumulation typically occurs after age 40, and most individuals older than 60 have diffuse calcification. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Cardiac Calcifications -- Etiology -- Valvular Calcifications
retrieved_docs.content: Age-related degeneration is the most common cause of calcification of the valves. However, patients with disturbance in calcium and phosphate hemodynamics (hypercalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, and hyperparathyroidism, as well as end-stage renal disease) are more prone to develop valvular calcifications. [4]

retrieved_docs.content: hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي) reflect abnormal calcium accumulation within the choroidal stroma. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common locus for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether hemorrhagic, inflammatory, traumatic, dystrophic, detachment‑related, or depositional—is essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate management, and optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: (ورم مخاطي أذيني) with a risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, and systemic atherosclerotic conditions (peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis) that manifest as aortic calcification and contribute to cerebral hypoperfusion. Collectively, these relationships illustrate an integrated network in which the heart serves as the primary site for electrophysiologic monitoring, invasive diagnostics, mechanical circulatory support, rhythm‑management device therapy, and surgical interventions, while its anatomical constituents and associated diseases are systematically linked to procedural hierarchies and systemic risk factors, underscoring the importance of comprehensive cardiac knowledge for optimal patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2261
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is classified as a form of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, and this broader category of disease is also referred to in Arabic as تكلس أبهري (aortic calcification). This mapping highlights that the atherosclerotic changes occurring in the abdominal aorta are part of the systemic peripheral and visceral atherosclerotic process and are recognized across languages as aortic calcification.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1238
retrieved_docs.content: malformation of the choroid (الأَتْلامُ وِعائِيَّةُ الشَّكل بالمشيمية), partial perimacular choroidal calcification (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and hereditary choroidal calcification (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي). Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroid is a central site for diverse pathological processes, and recognizing the specific type of choroidal involvement—whether inflammatory, scar‑forming, degenerative, traumatic, metastatic, or congenital—is essential for accurate diagnosis, targeted therapy, and optimal patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 971
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is considered a manifestation of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis and is found in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis). Peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which is also referred to by the Arabic term تكلس أبهري (aortic calcification), is identified as an associated condition that contributes to the risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion. Moreover, the risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion is linked as an associated condition of an atrial mucinous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Together, these relationships highlight how atherosclerotic disease of the abdominal aorta and broader peripheral/visceral atherosclerosis can impact cerebral perfusion and may be relevant in the clinical context of cardiac tumors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 579
retrieved_docs.content: and replacement of a cardiac pacemaker with a dual‑chamber device. From a systemic perspective, the condition “Risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion” is associated with peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which maps to aortic calcification, highlighting the link between systemic atherosclerotic disease and localized cardiac calcific changes. The same risk condition is also associated with the atrial mucous tumor, indicating a potential cerebrovascular complication linked to this specific atrial pathology. Collectively, these relationships illustrate how chronic cardiac diseases, structural heart lesions, systemic vascular risk factors, diagnostic and therapeutic cardiac procedures, device management strategies, and associated clinical conditions are interwoven, providing a structured framework essential for accurate diagnosis, appropriate device therapy, and comprehensive patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 1 -- Community 215
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis, also referred to by the Arabic term تكلس أبهري (aortic calcification), is classified within peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis and is located in the abdominal portion of the aorta (aorta abdominalis). This vascular pathology is linked to systemic consequences such as an increased risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, a condition also associated with atrial mucosal tumors (ورم مخاطي أذيني). The aorta, including its anatomy, histology, innervation, and growth‑and‑development characteristics, serves as the direct procedural site for major vascular surgeries (aortic resection, replacement, anastomosis) and for peripheral vascular bypass, with the abdominal aorta and the femoral artery (arteria femoralis) specifically noted as bypass targets. Imaging of the aorta is represented by the visual entity صورة الأبهر. In the orthopedic domain, the clavicle (klíční kost) is a central structure within the shoulder‑arm region and is the direct

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 2449
retrieved_docs.content: The Vascular System is identified as the anatomical site linked to both اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية (renal vascular disorder) and تسلخ الشرايين (arterial erosion). These conditions involve pathological changes within the blood vessels, with اضطراب الأوعية الكلوية affecting the renal vasculature and تسلخ الشرايين involving damage to arterial walls. Recognizing that both disorders share the Vascular System as their underlying anatomic substrate underscores the central role of vascular health in the development and management of renal vascular disease and arterial erosion, and highlights the need for comprehensive vascular assessment and care in patients presenting with these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1785
retrieved_docs.content: The risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion is closely linked to peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, a condition that is mapped to تكلس أبهري (aortic calcification). In addition, this risk is associated with ورم مخاطي أذيني (atrial mucinous tumor). Together, the presence of extensive atherosclerotic disease and aortic calcification, as well as the occurrence of an atrial mucinous tumor, can increase the likelihood of compromised cerebral blood flow, underscoring the need for vigilant cardiovascular and neurological assessment in patients with these conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2634
retrieved_docs.content: (تنكس حصوي) and choroidal atrophy (تَنَكُّسُ المَشيمِيَّة), while localized calcification around the optic disc (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي) represents a specific form of mineral deposition. Finally, choroidal scarring (ندبة مشيمية شبكية) denotes fibrotic remodeling after injury or inflammation. Collectively, these entities underscore the choroidea’s central role in ocular disease, highlighting the need for careful clinical assessment and targeted management of choroidal involvement across diverse pathological contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2115
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is a disease process that is found in the abdominal aorta, indicating that the abdominal aorta serves as the site of this atherosclerotic lesion.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 165
retrieved_docs.content: disease, heart murmur (نفخة قلبية), and atrial myxoma (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Atrial myxoma and other cardiac sources can cause ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion through embolic events, a process also related to peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis that manifests as aortic calcification. Together, these interrelated concepts illustrate the comprehensive landscape of cardiac structural disease, vascular risk factors, diagnostic modalities, ECG technologies, remote monitoring, and therapeutic device interventions essential for patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 145
retrieved_docs.content: the heart is represented by an atrial mucous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني); this tumor is clinically connected to a risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, reflecting possible embolic or hemodynamic complications. Aortic calcification (تكلس أبهري) is mapped to peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which in turn corresponds to abdominal aortic atherosclerosis located in the abdominal aorta (aorta abdominalis); peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis is identified as an associated condition for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, underscoring its systemic impact. Finally, srdce is noted to have a location termed ذَبْحَةُ برنزميتال, completing the network that integrates cardiac diagnostics, therapeutic device management, related inflammatory, neoplastic, and vascular pathologies, and their broader systemic consequences.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 135
retrieved_docs.content: choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين), and hemorrhagic choroidal detachment (انفصال مشيمية العين النزفي). Calcific processes are noted as calcific degeneration (تنكس حصوي), partial choroidal calcification around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُليمة، جزئي), and total choroidal calcification around the macula (حثل المشيمية حول الحُلَيمَة، الكلي). Hereditary and structural abnormalities include genetic choroidal hemorrhage (حَثَلُ المشيمية الوراثي), retinal‑choroidal scar (ندبة مشيمية شبكية), choroidal rupture, partial peripapillary choroidal sclerosis, total peripapillary choroidal sclerosis, hereditary choroidal sclerosis, and chorioretinal scar. Collectively, these entities illustrate that the choroidea is a common site of hemorrhage, scar formation, dystrophy, inflammation, infection, trauma, postoperative change, and metastatic involvement. Recognizing this extensive spectrum and its hierarchical organization aids clinicians in accurate diagnosis, epidemiologic tracking, and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 1 -- Community 177
retrieved_docs.content: include aortic calcification (تكلس أبهري), cardiac murmur, and atrial mucous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis (تكلس أبهري) is recognized as an associated condition that raises the risk of ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, linking systemic vascular disease to potential cerebral hypoperfusion. The clinical condition “risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion” is specifically associated with ورم مخاطي أذيني, indicating that patients with this atrial tumor may be predisposed to compromised cerebral blood flow. Overall, the knowledge base integrates heart structure, related diseases, systemic vascular risk, and a comprehensive set of procedures—from invasive biopsies and valve excisions to device implantation, revision, removal, resuscitation, and advanced imaging—providing a unified framework for integrated cardiovascular patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 1 -- Community 195
retrieved_docs.content: Abdominal aortic atherosclerosis is described as a manifestation of peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis, which are forms of arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي) and are also linked to aortic calcification (تكلس أبهري). Arteriosclerosis is a child of generalized arteriosclerosis (التصلب العصيدي المعمم) and belongs to the Blood, Heart and Circulation domain, itself a subcategory of chronic unspecified ischemic heart disease (مرض قلبي إقفاري مزمن، غير محدد). Vascular ischemia, cardiovascular disorder (اضطراب قلبي وعائي), and cardiovascular arteriosclerotic disease (داء تصلب الشرايين القلبي الوعائي) are related to arteriosclerosis. Peripheral and visceral atherosclerosis increase the risk for ineffective cerebral tissue perfusion, highlighting potential cerebrovascular complications, which are also associated with atrial mucous tumor (ورم مخاطي أذيني). Clinically, arteriosclerosis is associated with systolic murmur (النفخة الانقباضية), below‑knee amputation (بتر تحت الركبة), routine
